mission.title: Install and Configure Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS)
mission.description: Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) is a managed Kubernetes service that simplifies the process of running Kubernetes on AWS. It provides a highly available and scalable Kubernetes control plane, making it ideal for production workloads. Key trade-offs include reliance on AWS infrastructure and potential costs associated with managed services.

steps.title: Install the AWS CLI
steps.description:
The AWS CLI is required to interact with AWS services, including EKS. Ensure you have the latest version installed.

```bash

pip install awscli --upgrade --user

```

> Use this command to install or upgrade AWS CLI.

steps.title: Create an EKS Cluster
steps.description:
This command creates a new EKS cluster with the specified Kubernetes version, node count, and region.

```bash

aws eks create-cluster --name my-cluster --kubernetes-version 1.31 --role-arn arn:aws:iam::YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID:role/EKS-Cluster-Role --resources-vpc-config subnetIds=subnet-12345678,subnet-87654321

```

> Replace YOUR_ACCOUNT_ID with your actual AWS account ID.

steps.title: Update kubeconfig
steps.description:
This command updates your kubeconfig file to use the newly created EKS cluster.

```bash

aws eks update-kubeconfig --name my-cluster

```

> This allows kubectl to interact with your EKS cluster.

steps.title: Verify Cluster Nodes
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the nodes in your EKS cluster to ensure they are running.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

```

> You should see your nodes listed with a status of 'Ready'.

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
Configure autoscaling and monitoring for your EKS cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/master/cluster-autoscaler/cloudprovider/aws/examples/cluster-autoscaler-template.yaml

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aws/amazon-cloudwatch-agent/main/config/amazon-cloudwatch-agent-configmap.yaml

```

> This sets up cluster autoscaler.
> This sets up CloudWatch monitoring.

uninstall.title: Delete the EKS Cluster
uninstall.description:
This command deletes the EKS cluster and all associated resources. Be aware that this will result in data loss and orphaned resources.

```bash

aws eks delete-cluster --name my-cluster

```

uninstall.title: Cleanup Orphaned Resources
uninstall.description:
After deleting the cluster, manually check and delete any orphaned resources such as load balancers and persistent volumes.

```bash

aws ec2 describe-load-balancers

aws ec2 delete-load-balancer --load-balancer-arn YOUR_LOAD_BALANCER_ARN

```

> Check for any remaining load balancers.
> Replace with actual load balancer ARN.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the EKS Cluster
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you backup your data and drain the nodes if necessary. This command upgrades the EKS cluster to the specified version.

```bash

kubectl drain NODE_NAME --ignore-daemonsets

aws eks update-cluster-version --name my-cluster --kubernetes-version 1.31

kubectl get nodes

```

> Replace NODE_NAME with the actual node name.
> This upgrades the cluster to version 1.31.
> Verify that the nodes are updated and in 'Ready' status.

troubleshooting.title: Error: Unable to connect to the EKS cluster
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the kubeconfig is not set up correctly or if the cluster is not running.

**Fix:**
Run 'aws eks update-kubeconfig --name my-cluster' to update your kubeconfig.

troubleshooting.title: Error: No resources found in namespace kube-system
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the necessary components may not be deployed.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the EKS cluster is fully created and components are deployed.

troubleshooting.title: Error: Cluster autoscaler not scaling nodes
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may happen if the IAM role does not have the necessary permissions.

**Fix:**
Ensure the IAM role has permissions for autoscaling and EC2 actions.

troubleshooting.title: Error: Insufficient capacity in your availability zone
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that there are not enough resources available in the specified availability zone.

**Fix:**
Try launching the cluster in a different availability zone or increase the instance type.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new Kubernetes features such as PodSecurity admission and improved logging capabilities.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecation of legacy API versions for several resources.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 1.30, ensure to review the changes in API versions and update manifests accordingly.

resolution.summary: You should see the output of 'kubectl get nodes' showing your nodes in 'Ready' status, indicating that the cluster is operational.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the required tools and resources before starting the installation.
